reasoning: The cash assets of commercial banks (mainly consisting of reserve balances at the Fed) have been rapidly dropping since July 2025, falling from around 3400 billion to below 2900 billion by early November. This strong downward trend is largely due to the continuation of the Fed’s quantitative tightening (QT) operations fully impacting reserves now that the ON RRP facility has drained. A purely extrapolated random walk with this current negative drift would suggest exceedingly low probabilities of an increase over longer horizons. However, bank reserves are likely approaching their "ample" threshold (around 10-12% of GDP), which increases the likelihood that the Fed could slow or end QT during the forecast period. Furthermore, seasonal factors (cash assets typically rise in Q1 relative to Q4, as seen in Jan/Feb 2025) provide a structural counterweight to the downward drift by February 2026. Following the explicit instruction to center distant probabilities closer to 0.5 due to escalating policy and macroeconomic uncertainty, I apply a structurally increasing uncertainty band: P1 (1-week) sits at 0.30 reflecting the dominant immediate trend but with week-to-week volatility; P2 (1-month) rises to 0.38 reflecting compounding uncertainty about the persistence of rapid QT drains; P3 (3-months) nears a coin flip at 0.46, given the significant likelihood of a policy shift or seasonal Q1 banking system bounce.
